To justify my response, I'll refer back to key pieces of evidence:
First, the articles consistently discuss the importance of proper antibiotic prophylaxis for surgical site infections, highlighting how these can greatly reduce infection rates. Specifically, in relation to clean-contaminated wounds (which would fall under "clean" + parts of "contaminated"), we see consistent reporting indicating these carry some risk beyond just clean wounds.
Second, the data suggests variability based on specific conditions like diabetic status, immune function, etc. But importantly, even outside of special subsets, clean-contaminated wounds remain a category distinct from completely clean ones.
Third, looking directly at percentages - though precise figures aren't frequently cited here - we note ranges that correlate well with B being less than 10%, yet implying a notable occurrence above what you'd expect purely from clean classifications.
Thus, basing our estimate on general trends described and typical expectations set out in the field, option B (<10%) seems quite plausible representing the percentage seen clinically despite effective prophylactic regimens aimed specifically at minimizing further risk. This aligns closely with common understanding in surgical settings today concerning balanced approach towards avoiding unnecessary treatments vs achieving needed protection. Therefore,
